sentences.text: The present name is Solly Irving, S-O-L-L-Y I-R-V-I-N-G.
My name at birth was Szlama Zelman Judensznaider, J-U-D-E-N-E-N-S-Z-N-A-I-D-E-R.
Szlama, which is, uh, Solomon, uh, Zelman, Z-E-L-M-A-N.
Middle name is Zelman, yes.
No.
Officially, at the moment, is 11 of August 1930.
But my original actual date since I found my birth certificate, a copy of it, was the 12th of August.
I was actually born the 12th of August 1930.
At the moment, I'm presently 66, coming on 67.
Ryki, Poland.
R, uh, R-I-K-Y.
Mm.
It's actually is between Warsaw and Lublin, exactly halfway between Warsaw and Lublin.
Chana Necha.
Uh, I should imagine it as C-H-A-N-A and Necha, N-E-C-H-A.
Simple.
My father was, eh, Icek in Polish and in Hebrew was Yisrael Yitzhak, Israel, eh, Isaac.
Oh, of them?
I would have to say, would be three years old when I was taken for my first haircut.
You know, it's-- traditionally, in among the Hasidic sect, the boys don't get their hair cut until they're three years old.
In fact, my three grandchildren had the same-- same custom, yeah.
That's what I remember my first impression of my parents.
To be honest, I'm ashamed to say, I don't remember very much.
It's completely blank what they were doing-- I mean, what they were doing professionally I've learned since.
But what they were like as people-- I mean, as all parents, I had fear of my father.
And if anything, we stood under my mother for protection.
That used to be the thing.
Although he never hit us, but we always, always expected him.
And the same thing my mother-- I was always told my mother was always a ill person.
Yet, when the Germans came in, she was one of the strongest in the family.
What her ailment were, was, uh, I'm not sure.
I had four sisters.
I was the only boy.
I had four sisters, three before me and one after me.
And the oldest one was Rivka.
And the second one was Leah.
The third one was Hinda.
And the fourth one was Mendel.
Mendel was the youngest.
I have no recollection of their ages or anything.
Not really.
I remember my oldest sister coming to fetch me from school 'cause school used to finish the year of-- going to school was only one year before the war broke out.
And she used to collect me from school mid-day and give me some sandwiches or lunch and take me on to for the next lessons or next school.
No.
As far as I know, very good-- being the only boy, spoiled no doubt.
But I don't have, uh, very good recollections on exactly what else, what it was like.
The last we lived in a house, a brick built house that I remember.
I don't remember how many bedrooms.
It had a backyard, yes, not so much of the garden.
It was a backyard, exactly what was there I don't know.
I remember that because I remember my father when the-- during the war, we dug up and hid certain valuables and also in the cellar.
In the cellar, we used hid, to hide the small things.
Valuables, jewelry.
No, I didn't help in burying them.
Children were always been to-- told to stay out of the way.
No, I didn't.
Uh, I certainly didn't and none of the family did that were survived.
Because, um, I came across a cousin, with the name Chaim Judensznajder.
He was the, um, the bro-- the nephew of my-- my father, one of them, anyway.
And, uh, he told me he jumped from the first train, from the ghetto was liquidated.
And he was trying to hide in our house.
And he told me that everything was dug up within hours of being liquidated [INAUDIBLE] the Jews taking out everything [INAUDIBLE].
They knew who to go-- also we were, we were in business.
My father was in grain business together with his brothers.
And I think he had-- they had partners, as well.
So, obviously, the people knew, yeah.
Well, I've learned since it was going to Sobibor.
I always felt, for many years, I thought they would went Treblinka, which was at Lublin.
But since Martin Gilson, Matthew Gilbert, called the of and he gives clear all the towns and the dates and everything.
So since then, I-- I assumed that it was Sobibor.
Eh--
Both.
What the-- if you tell me the good ones.
Well, the-- the-- the good ones is because, um, as a boy, we played, going to cheder, you know cheder.
But the unpleasant memories is when I first went to school, and I was abused as a Jew, as a Jewish boy.
And it was terrible.
And originally my mother didn't want me to go to a mixed school.
But my father, I suppose, being in business he wanted me to grow up also and to have a better education.
Because the cheder only taught Jewish religious things, most of them anyway.
So before-- year before the war broke out, my father bribed the headmaster, and, uh, he took me to school.
And the schools were always outside the center of the town, certainly by-- by church, mostly by churches.
And, uh, one day I was attacked coming home from school by boys and rubbed my mouth with ham, abused me, hit me.
And-- and I came home, obviously, cried for my father.
Next day my father took me to the school, to the headmaster and explained what happened.
And the headmaster took me around in their parade, assembly.
And he said, show me who did this to you.
Of course, I couldn't tell him.
But he pulled me by my ear.
He didn't take me by my hand.
I mean, I couldn't have been that much bigger, but I was a young-- I'm small today.
So I was much, much smaller then.
I could have only been what, eight, nine years old.
No.
None at all.
None at all.
I was the only boy sitting with a beret in school, with my payot-- you know what payot are-- tucked under.
So, obviously, I was a target for them.
Uh, no.

In fact, my mother insisted the school caps, in those days in Polish schools, was a-- was almost like a-- if you ever seen a Polish soldier, they have the-- like a cross on the top sticking out there.
And so my mother objected.
So I was the only one wearing with a beret instead of a school cap.
No.
No.
It's completely out of my-- I was only there a year, if that, before broke-- war broke out.
Oh, we played games, uh, going with my parents in the summer months to, um, to the forest for holidays.

Well, the biggest game in those, it used to be like-- from a piece of wire, we used to make like a-- and for wheel, take a wheel from the bicycle, and roll it along, and catch it in that piece of wire.
So it shouldn't fall over.
There was an, also, um, uh, here you have cricket.
We didn't play cricket.
I played football, tried to play football.
We also had a game, it was a piece of wood, short piece of wood with two ends sharpened down and used to hit it, how far it could-- it would travel, or coins against the, the wall, you know.
They had names.
I don't remember them.

One-- one particular [? pallent-- ?] [? pallent, ?] used to be called, a piece of wood with the-- with the edges, eh, sharpened down so you could hit it, and have fun-- I used to -- [INAUDIBLE] used to be called [? pallent. ?]
Boys, mostly.
But we-- I didn't play much with the girls.
We very much, uh, of the Orthodox sects, and I don't remember playing with girls, to be honest, even at that young age, yeah.
Yes, yes.
Ryki, was yes-- as far as I know, yes, it was very Orthodox.
Many, I wouldn't-- yeah, there was one large synagogue, which belonged to the town.
And then each sect had their own [NON-ENGLISH], yeah.
Quite a few.
I don't know exactly how many but quite a few.
Because each offshoot had their own-- the rabbi's offshoot had their own.
And if you belong to a [NON-ENGLISH].
If you belong to the shoe menders, they had their own synagogue.
The tailors had their own synagogue.
So each one had their own, their own little pla-- parish.
It was a place called [NON-ENGLISH], which was [? near Pol-- ?] near Warsaw.
And that's an offshoot of [?
Gehr ?], or the famous [?
Gehr ?] was an offshoot where one of the relations, a son, son-in-law, nephew or somebody established a sect, and that's what, uh, we went to.
Yes.
You'll see in the picture, I used to wear little black coat from the time-- from the time I was five-- black coat and black socks.
The, uh, coat, I remember distinctly when I started learning chumash, my grandfather, my father's side, gave me that.
And also a gartel, you know [INAUDIBLE] where a dividing, uh, belt on top of the coat.
It's-- it's not called a belt.
It's called a gartel, a divider between the heavenly and the bottom part of the body, spiritually.
Sure, uh, in exactly what way I don't remember.
But I remember when I started learning chumash that was, uh, that was something.
The party, it went on for hours, and after I had to give a speech, um, which I was thankful, told what to say and how to say it, you know.
And, um, it was almost like a bar mitzvah today.
Yes, I do on the religious side.
Yes, yes, I do.
Yeah, oh yeah, of course, always Purim, and always it was customary twice a year, you got new clothes-- Pesach and Sukkos.
You always got something new-- however poor we were then how poor or how rich we were, we always had new things.
Well, it was-- it came naturally.
Well, whether-- if you needed shoes, you got shoes.
If you needed a-- a pair of trousers, you got a pair of trousers, or shirt, whatever.
Yes, yes, yes.
They also got presents.
And they were--
I would imagine so.
I don't know exactly what the-- my oldest sister went to gymnasium, which is all year high school.
Um, I seem to remember she almost finished, because she worked for my father's business parlor at the time.
What else she did after, I don't know.
Yeah, yeah.
As far as I know, they were still in-- learning.
Whether they-- I don't think they went to-- to high schools at that time.
Maybe they went to girl's [? sams ?] or something like that, yeah.
No, no.
I was always tested by my father on a Friday night-- excuse me-- what I've learned.
Actually he paid for it, because he wanted to know what I learned.
It wasn't, um-- education, like even today, a lot of it is private.
It's not state aided.
It certainly wasn't in Poland.
I'm sure it wasn't state aided.
Well, I'm sure I didn't.
I'm sure I didn't, but I was very friendly with the, um, the son of our town rabbis.
And we always learned together.
And [INAUDIBLE], I went to his father, and he came to my father always to be tested.
It was mostly on a Friday night or Shabbos depending on when there was a-- it was Friday night.
In the summer, it was Shabbos afternoon.
Yes, I remember the walk going through to him, yes, yeah.
Well, I don't remember exactly the detail, but I would imagine when you get up in the morning you had certain prayers.
I'm sure I must have gone with my father if I was old enough to go with him to [?
Shul ?], uh, like so many today.
And-- and then went to school, [NON-ENGLISH] school, came home for lunch, met my sis-- my sis, met by my sister, gave me some lunch, and take me on to the next stage of my education.
So didn't have much time to-- for play, but we always found [? time, ?] with boys we always found time.
I'm sure we found time to play, mostly winters, skating on our hills, or sliding down some little hill, and making up, um, slates or whatever, you know.
I'm sure we [INAUDIBLE] doing the same thing as boys do today, maybe more modern way today you got computers.
So they sit in those and watch television.
We never had television or radio.
I don't remember having radio at home.
And, um, with whom did you play?
Boys in the cheder.
This the year of the school, nah.
They, they were mostly non-Jews anyway.
And the Jewish boys I used to meet in the cheder.
We used to play together.
I would think so.
I don't know how close we were.
I'm sure we were.
In those days, most families were close, rich or poor.
Family was something that we could learn today, from today, yeah.
I don't remember, um, when he came into power.
I wouldn't, I was too young to remember.
The only time I remember when this came about was, um, when war broke out, and the planes kept coming over our town.
And people are running to hide.
And they used to come down so low, you could see the pilots, almost their eyeballs and shooting at the people.
That's my first recollection.
I can't.
I'm sure I must have done.
I'm sure, but who exactly individually, I-- I wouldn't know.
I don't remember.
It's a terrible thing to, uh, to have gone through a life and not remembering many things with them in childhood.
I used to be so jealous when I came across recent years of friends, say, oh, I've known them so long.
I've known them so long, and they did things together.
I used to be terribly envious of them.
And now, when I think of it, I also know people now that I've known 50 years, people I met up in the camps [INAUDIBLE] and since the camps, yeah.
So it's-- it's a long time.
Yet, um, when you think about-- I was still-- I was envious.
There were two very close friends of mine.
Whenever we got together, used to talk about holidays that you have to get together from school and the things they used to do in school.
And I always used to think, if only I had someone who remembered me from home, yeah, what I was like.
My friends were here.
It was one of my closest friends, Sidney Wagner.
He lives up the road here and David Halo.
These are some of my closest friends, from English boys.
I'm talking about English bo-- boys.
Of course, many from my group that I know and--
The 458 Society, Holocaust survivors.
We very close.

And also the boys that I've spent the time with in the different hostels since I've came to England or met [? up in ?] Theresienstadt or met up during the war-- my longest friend, another one is still alive.
It's two actually.
One lives in Australia.
His name is, um, Jack Feingold, Fein.
He calls himself Fein, used to be [?
Feingzer, ?] calls himself Fein, that I visited uh, on a year ago in Australia.
He's been here several time--he actually came with me to England.
And he left for Australia he had two uncles that survived the war and took him over.
They went after the war to Australia.
And they took him, and brought him to Melbourne.
Unfortunately, when I went this time-- I remember that his uncles from the camps, early camp days-- and they weren't alive anymore.
So I was very sorry.
One dies within weeks before I got there.
So I was rather upset.
I thought they might have been able to tell me about my father, because they were much older, and they knew my father.
But I was too late.
I came another one that came across, much younger [INAUDIBLE] my age, who said to me, he remembered me from home and he met my father.
And I said, what he look like?
He says, like all Jews with a beard and a certain type cap.
You know it didn't help very much.
German army.
Well, uh, when the planes came over, and after they-- it-- it-- it died down a bit, we, um, his children-- my parents took me away to another town called [?
Jelehova. ?] And-- and there we've spent a few months.
And when we came back German occupation [INAUDIBLE] and it wasn't too bad.
My father carried on business with him.
I remember one year the army provided them with an escort and a lorry to go to Lublin, to bring back flour for Pesach.
I remember that.
When exactly it was I don't remember.
But so we must have carried them.
He must have had some-- some-- made some friends in the German occupation now, because I have a telegram which was sent via that person.

Now we had no-- no way of doing it at [INAUDIBLE] unless he had, um, somebody he knew in the occupation forces that, um, would have given that to help to send a telegram to my uncle to Palestine who lived in [?
Haifa then. ?]
--to say all was well.
He left in 1933-34.
Yaacov Finkelman.
He was the brother of my mother, one-- one of the brothers.
He-- well, he-- he-- lived in-- in-- Palestine, in Israel.
Of course, we never spoke much about it.
I tried to talk to him many times about it.
He always avoided talking.
He felt very guilty of-- because apparently he, uh-- he had-- obviously things were tough in Palestine in those days.
And my father, I believe, asked him what he should do, whether he should to come to Palestine or not.
And he advised him not to.
You see, things were hard.
He had to work as a laborer, in the building material on the-- the land.
And, uh, he knew that my father, [PAUSES] or my mother, rather, was the oldest of-- of the family.
And there was still sisters of my mother that weren't married.
And she sort of looked after them.
So he felt a bit guilty for advising my father, not directly, but hinted to him that it wasn't the place for him.
Very good, almost-- almost like a father to me.
And in fact when I, when I was demobbed from the Israeli army in 1949 he wanted me very much to come and live with him.
He had two daughters and, uh, obviously his wife And his wife, particularly-- she was like a mother to me.
They wanted me very much to come live with him and for me to go to college, get an education, which I never had.
But, um, I felt it was wrong of me to stay on, because things were very, very tough for them even.
He was in the army.
He was trying to bring up two daughters himself.
And, uh, things were not easy, and I felt that I should give them a little bit rest.
Minucha.
Sa-- one-- the oldest one is Sarah and the second one is Shoshana.
And we still very close.
Although they marry and aunt and uncle died.
And-- But I'm close to my cousins.
And we're very much in touch with each other.
Well, exactly what happened-- I mean, I'm sure after awhile they stopped coming over to find, and they occupied Poland.
We came back into town, and things carried on as normal.
And then, um, sudden they created a ghetto.
Ghetto must have been-- must have been-- ghetto must have been I would reckon '40, '41 they establish a ghetto.
And, uh, and they designate a certain area.
We were lucky that our house was in the ghetto, so we didn't have to move out.
But a lot of people came in.
And there's one, uh, one man in Israel, at the moment, called Moishe Oppenheim that lived-- he spent, his family, spent time in our house.
No, I'm sure their others, but I don't remember.
Well, until-- till the war, it was only just our own family, but then, obviously, my mother's sisters and brother came in and this family, Oppenheim, came in.
And there must have been others, as well.
It was quite a lot.
Yes, of course.
Not very good.
Not very good.
I don't know how long the ghetto was established.
But, um-- but then suddenly town was surrounded one morning.
It was a commotion and town was surrounded.
And then German lorries came in, surrounded the center of the town, and took everybody into-- into the mid-- square of the town and started transporting them to-- away.
No, we didn't, but my father, somehow, managed to bribe  a guard to let me through.
No, I don't remember the guards' name now.
No, my father-- I remember my father coming to-- to the house, and pulling me by my hand.
And he said, I want you to escape, and to [INAUDIBLE] I think there should be somebody of the family left.
And I will tell you where.
Make for such and such farm, and that I've given the men certain valuables and he will look after you.
So he thought, obviously.
And so I escaped.
[PAUSES] Don't ask me how I managed to escape.
I don't really know how.
There was, um, Germans going up and down the road.
And I was dressed-- I must have been dressed in the almost in the uniform that I wore with the black coat.
And so I didn't look Polish.
Why they didn't stop me, I don't know.
And then with minutes escaping a sister, my second older sister, Leah, came running and we managed-- met up with me.
And she managed to-- to escape with me.
All I know is that the man, the farmer that I made for, my father told me to-- to go to eventually came home that night and he told me, he so.
He took a horse and cart.
They were ordered to bring horse and carts to town to transport the Jewish people.
Those who could walk had to walk, and those who couldn't, had the older or the infirm put on the tracks and the horse and cart to be transported to the station.
